item.description: Bloomberg satellite tracking data, cited by TankerTrackers.com co-founder Samir Madani, revealed that Iran has shipped at least 11-12 million barrels of crude oil through the Strait of Hormuz since February 28 — directly contradicting IRGC declarations of a "complete blockade" and "not one litre" of oil passage. Iran's actual posture appears to be a selective embargo: IRGC-linked or US/Israel-associated tankers are targeted, while Iranian state exports continue. The gap between Iran's rhetorical maximalism and operational reality is significant. Separately, Trump told CBS News he is considering "seizing control of the Strait of Hormuz" — the first time the US president explicitly floated physical US military control of the world's most critical energy chokepoint. Iran's Foreign Ministry spokesman Esmail Baghaei warned tankers to "be very careful" and insisted targeting US military assets was "legitimate under international law." Iran has also warned it will make "maximum use" of Hormuz as a strategic resource. The real-world data suggests both sides are managing escalation carefully even as their public statements point toward all-out confrontation.

item.title: Trump: "Death, Fire and Fury" if Iran Blocks Hormuz — Larijani Threatens Trump's Life
item.description: Day 12 of the Iran war produced its sharpest rhetorical exchange yet. Trump threatened Iran with "death, fire and fury" if it continues blocking the Strait of Hormuz, while Iran's IRGC commander declared: "Not one litre of oil will leave the Middle East until US and Israeli attacks cease." Iran's parliament speaker Larijani simultaneously posted a formal Hormuz ultimatum in 6 languages — "The Strait will be a strait of peace and prosperity for all, or a strait of defeat and suffering for warmongers" — then followed minutes later with a direct personal threat to Trump on X: "Be careful, so that you won't get eliminated." The Larijani threat was the first direct personal threat to a sitting US president during the conflict. Iran also said it intends to "fight as long as needed" even if the US stops unilaterally. Meanwhile US Energy Secretary Chris Wright briefly posted that the US Navy had "successfully escorted an oil tanker through the Strait" before deleting the message — but oil markets had already responded, with Brent crude falling toward $82 before fresh Iranian missile strikes reversed the drop. The war of statements is escalating faster than the war of missiles.

item.title: US Tells Israel to Stop Striking Iran's Energy Infrastructure — Axios: Washington Wants Iran's Oil Sector Intact for Post-War
item.description: The United States has asked Israel to halt its strikes on Iranian energy infrastructure, Axios reported March 10 citing three sources familiar with the matter. Washington delivered the message at a senior political level and directly to IDF Chief of Staff Eyal Zamir. The Trump administration cited three reasons, including a strategic goal: preserving Iran's oil sector for post-war cooperation and rapid Hormuz reopening. This is a significant crack in the US-Israel targeting consensus — it directly contradicts Israel's "maximum damage before Trump halts" doctrine confirmed by Reuters the same day. The US wants Iran's energy capacity preserved as an economic lever for post-war diplomacy; Israel wants to degrade Iran's economic and military power base permanently. Separately, the White House announced the US military is now moving to "dismantle Iran's missile production" — Phase 3 targeting beyond launchers to manufacturing facilities. And India's External Affairs Minister Jaishankar held a "detailed" call with Araghchi (March 10 evening), with India positioning itself as a potential diplomatic bridge — India imports significant Iranian crude and has economic stakes in a rapid resolution. Israel simultaneously approved a special "war budget" of tens of billions of shekels — Netanyahu and FM Smotrich signed off — suggesting Israel intends to keep striking regardless of US preferences. The energy strike disagreement is the clearest public evidence yet of a divergent endgame: Trump wants a negotiated outcome with Iran's economy intact enough to reopen oil quickly; Netanyahu wants permanent degradation of Iran's power base.

item.title: IDF Operating to Inflict "Maximum Damage" Before Trump Halts War — Israel Races Against Clock
item.description: Israel's military is deliberately operating to inflict as much damage as possible before the window for further strikes on Iran closes, under the explicit assumption that US President Donald Trump could end the war at any moment, Reuters reported March 10 citing a source familiar with Israel's war plans. Israeli Foreign Minister Gideon Saar confirmed the logic publicly: "We will continue until the minute that we, and our partners, think that it is appropriate to stop." Saar noted Trump said the war would not end this week, but declined to offer any timeline himself. The revelation confirms a strategic asymmetry at the heart of the US-Israel coalition: the US is managing economic and diplomatic pressure to find an off-ramp, while Israel is racing to degrade as much of Iran's nuclear, ballistic missile, and proxy infrastructure as possible before the US enforces a halt. Tehran residents described overnight bombardment as "the most intense of the entire war" — "It was like hell. They were bombing everywhere, every part of Tehran. My children are afraid to sleep." Israel said it struck a weapons research-and-development target in Tehran alongside multiple other sites. The implications: Israel's calculus is not synchronized with Trump's. This directly explains the intensifying strike tempo despite Trump's "very soon" language — Israel is maximizing the window, not waiting for diplomatic resolution. Reuters noted investors are betting Trump will end the war before triggering a 1970s-style energy shock, with Brent having fallen from ~$120 to ~$92 on that expectation.

item.title: ADNOC Shuts World's 4th-Largest Refinery — 922,000 bpd Offline After Iranian Drone Strike on Ruwais
item.description: Abu Dhabi state oil giant ADNOC was forced to shut the Ruwais refinery — the world's fourth-largest single-site refinery — after an Iranian drone struck the Ruwais Industrial Complex, triggering a fire on March 10. The complex hosts ADNOC facilities capable of processing up to 922,000 barrels of crude oil per day and serves as the central hub for Abu Dhabi's downstream operations, including chemical, fertilizer, and industrial gas plants. Industry monitor IIR Energy confirmed ADNOC shut the lone crude distillation unit at its 417,000 bpd Ruwais Refinery 2 (West), with a plant-wide safety shutdown planned; Ruwais Refinery 1 (East, 400,000 bpd) had already been operating 10-20% below capacity since March 6 due to regional conflict. No injuries were reported. Ruwais is the largest refinery in the Middle East. Combined with Saudi Aramco's Ras Tanura hit March 2 (550,000 bpd offline) and Kuwait's production cuts, the Ruwais strike means the Gulf is now losing well over 1.5M bpd of refining capacity — separate from the Hormuz blockade impact on crude flows. The attack confirms Iran is systematically working through Gulf energy infrastructure even as IRGC publicly threatened that "not a single drop of oil" will reach the US or its allies while strikes continue. Oil at ~$92/bbl on March 11 markets, down from $120 peak — traders pricing in ceasefire, not this new refinery strike.

item.title: Iran Begins Laying Mines in Strait of Hormuz; US CENTCOM Destroys 16 Iranian Mine-Laying Vessels
item.description: Iran has begun physically laying mines in the Strait of Hormuz, the world's most critical energy chokepoint carrying ~20% of all crude oil, according to two people familiar with US intelligence (CNN, March 10). While the mining is not yet extensive — "a few dozen" mines laid in recent days — Iran still retains 80–90% of its small boats and mine-laying vessels, sources said, meaning it could feasibly scale rapidly. In direct response, US CENTCOM destroyed 16 Iranian mine-laying vessels "near the Strait of Hormuz" on Tuesday, per a Pentagon statement. Gen. Dan Caine (Chairman, Joint Chiefs) said CENTCOM "continues today to hunt and strike mine-laying vessels and mine storage facilities." Trump posted on Truth Social: "If Iran has put out any mines in the Hormuz Strait... we want them removed, IMMEDIATELY!" — simultaneously denying confirmed intelligence reports before acknowledging strikes against the vessels. Trump also said the US would use "the same technology deployed against drug traffickers to permanently eliminate any boat or ship attempting to mine the Hormuz Strait." Tally: US has now sunk or destroyed 50+ Iranian naval ships in the first 10 days of war (Caine). The Hormuz mining threshold matters because it transforms the blockade from operational/legal to physical and indiscriminate — sea mines kill neutral ships, trigger insurance total withdrawal, and activate international law of armed conflict provisions. The Strait has been effectively closed for 10+ days already, but actual mining removes the possibility of a quick diplomatic reopening; de-mining operations typically take weeks to months. Macron announced France is preparing a "purely defensive" Hormuz escort mission. EU shipping insurers have already cancelled Gulf war risk cover. 200+ tankers remain clustered at Gulf ports; production halted as storage fills across Iraq, Kuwait, UAE.

item.title: Donald Knuth Publishes "Claude's Cycles" — Claude Opus 4.6 Solves Open Graph Theory Problem He'd Worked on for Weeks
item.description: Donald Knuth — Stanford CS legend, Turing Award winner, and author of the multi-volume "The Art of Computer Programming" — published a paper titled "Claude's Cycles" (Feb 28, revised March 6, 2026) opening with "Shock! Shock!" after Claude Opus 4.6 cracked an open mathematical problem he had been working on for several weeks. The problem: decomposing a directed graph with m³ vertices into three Hamiltonian cycles for all m greater than 2. Knuth had solved the m=3 case; colleague Filip Stappers had verified solutions up to m=16 empirically, but no general construction existed. Stappers fed the exact problem to Claude Opus 4.6. Over 31 guided explorations in roughly one hour, Claude tested linear formulas, ran DFS searches, developed geometric frameworks, applied simulated annealing, independently recognized the problem's structure as a Cayley digraph, and ultimately derived a "serpentine" construction — which turned out to correspond to the classical modular m-ary Gray code. Claude derived it from scratch without knowing the name. The solution worked for all odd m from 3 to 101. Knuth himself then wrote the formal mathematical proof. He noted the problem for even m remains open. "It seems I'll have to revise my opinions about 'generative AI' one of these days" — from arguably the most respected computer scientist alive, this is a historically significant statement. The post sharing the paper accumulated 635,000+ views and 6,000 likes within hours of publication.

item.title: Google Deploys Gemini AI Agents Across Pentagon's 3 Million Employees for Unclassified Work
item.description: Google is rolling out Gemini AI agents to the Department of Defense's 3 million civilian and military employees, expanding its Pentagon AI footprint amid the ongoing Anthropic blacklist standoff. The agents will initially operate on unclassified networks, automating tasks like summarizing meeting notes, building budgets, and checking proposed actions against the national defense strategy. Eight pre-built agents launch immediately; DoD staff can also build custom agents using natural language via Google's "Agent Designer" (equivalent to Gems). Emil Michael, Under Secretary of Defense for Research and Engineering, confirmed talks are already underway to expand access to classified and top-secret networks. Context: 1.2 million DoD employees have already used Gemini via GenAI.mil, running 40 million prompts and uploading 4 million documents since December. The rollout comes directly after the Pentagon designated Anthropic a "supply chain risk" (March 3) and rushed deals with OpenAI and xAI — Google is now the largest AI agent deployment in the US military. Notably, Google quietly altered its own AI Principles on military use in early February, enabling this expansion after internal backlash over previous Pentagon work in 2018.

item.title: IDF Strikes Nuclear Laboratories in Tehran and Isfahan in Most Intensive Wave Yet — 170+ Munitions Deployed
item.description: Israeli Air Force fighter jets struck nuclear laboratories in Tehran as part of a wave using more than 170 munitions across Tehran, Isfahan, and Shiraz, according to the Alma Research and Education Center's daily war report (March 10, 18:00). The nuclear labs struck had previously been targeted during the June 2025 Twelve-Day War — their re-targeting signals the same or reconstituted facilities are still operational. Additional targets in the wave: a central IRGC headquarters responsible for launching UAVs (in Tehran), the IRGC Quds Force HQ in Tehran responsible for proxy operations and weapons transfers, a missile production and storage site in Isfahan (targeting Israeli aircraft), multiple Iranian air defense systems in Isfahan, the Shahramfar IRGC base in Sanandaj (Iranian Kurdistan), and infrastructure in Khorramabad used as gathering sites or weapons storage. Strikes also reported near Mehrabad Airport in Tehran. This represents the most systematic targeting of nuclear infrastructure since the war began Feb 28 — going beyond the June 2025 strikes in scope by hitting laboratories (not just centrifuge halls). The IAEA has been unable to access Iranian nuclear sites since the war began; Iran holds an estimated 440.9kg of 60%-enriched uranium at Isfahan, with the fate of the stockpile unknown.

item.title: Saudi Arabia Intercepts Six Ballistic Missiles Aimed at Prince Sultan Air Base
item.description: Saudi Arabia's Defence Ministry confirmed it intercepted and destroyed six ballistic missiles heading for Prince Sultan Air Base in the east of the country. The ministry also reported intercepting several drones, including two near the northeastern city of Hafar Al-Batin. The ministry did not specify the source of the projectiles. Prince Sultan Air Base is a key US military facility that has been used in the ongoing US-Israeli campaign against Iran. The intercepts mark a continuation of overnight Iranian strikes on Gulf military infrastructure as Day 12 transitions into Day 13 of the conflict.

item.title: UAE Air Defence Systems Responding to Active Missile Threat — Public Told to Shelter
item.description: The UAE's National Emergency Crisis and Disaster Management Authority confirmed at 21:31 GMT that "air defence systems are currently responding to a missile threat." The public was instructed to remain in a safe location and follow official guidance. The alert comes minutes after Israel's IDF announced an "additional wave" of strikes on Tehran (21:26 GMT), suggesting coordinated Iranian retaliation targeting Gulf neighbours. The UAE had previously intercepted 262 ballistic missiles and 1,475 drones since the war began on 28 February, with 9 drones hitting UAE soil on Day 12 alone.

item.title: Iran Police Chief: Would-Be Protesters Will Be Treated as 'the Enemy' — 'Fingers on the Trigger'
item.description: In direct response to Netanyahu's 21:08 GMT call for Iranians to rise up against the establishment, Iran Police Chief Ahmadreza Radan issued a stark warning on state TV: 'If someone comes at the enemy's bidding, we do not regard them as a protester or anything of the sort; we treat them as we would treat the enemy, and we deal with them in the same way we deal with the enemy.' He added: 'All our forces also have their fingers on the trigger, ready to defend the [Revolution].' BBC Persian notes Iran has recently seen nationwide anti-establishment protests (starting late December) that prompted the deadliest crackdown in the Islamic Republic's history — HRANA confirmed at least 7,000 killed. The two statements together (Netanyahu's uprising call + Radan's shoot-to-kill counter) define the high-stakes endgame tension entering Day 13.

item.title: Netanyahu to Iranian People: 'Remove Your Tyrants and Seize the Moment' — Regime Change Explicit
item.description: Israeli PM Netanyahu posted a direct address to the 'people of Iran' on X, explicitly calling for them to overthrow the Ayatollah regime. Key quotes: 'The Ayatollah is no more, and I know you don't want him replaced with another tyrant. So you must act. We are creating the conditions for you to do so.' Netanyahu added: 'In the coming days we will create the conditions for you to grasp your destiny' and 'When the time is right, and that time is fast approaching, we will pass the torch to you.' The post makes explicit what US/Israeli military action is ultimately aimed at — popular uprising to topple the Islamic Republic — and signals imminent escalation toward what Netanyahu frames as an endgame window. The 'in the coming days' language is the most direct regime-change timeline statement from either government.

item.title: Iran Formally Accuses Israel at UN of Deliberately Killing Four Diplomats in Beirut Hotel Strike
item.description: Iran's UN Ambassador Amir Saeid Iravani sent a formal letter to UN Secretary-General Antonio Guterres accusing Israel of the 'deliberate' and 'targeted' killing of four Iranian diplomats at the Ramada Hotel in central Beirut on Sunday. Iran says the diplomats had moved from their official residences to the hotel and were killed while serving as 'official representatives of a sovereign Member State.' The letter calls the strike 'a grave terrorist act and a serious breach of international law.' Israel's IDF countered that the five men killed were Quds Force (IRGC overseas operations) operatives conducting a secret meeting — not protected diplomats. Lebanese health ministry confirmed 4 killed, 10 injured. The formal UN complaint marks an escalation in the legal and diplomatic dimension of the war alongside the ongoing military campaign.
